coredata.dc:title: Potentiality of carbon quantum dots derived from chitin as a fluorescent sensor for detection of ClO−
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Qiwen
jing, Yi
Ni, Yeyan
Gao, Ruoshi
Zhou, Pingyan
coredata.dc:description: Chitin, a functional carbohydrate derived from waste products of seafood industry, contains abundant nitrogen, which makes it become a very suitable carbon precursor for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Demonstrated in this work was synthesizing CQDs using this N-enriched primary feed-stock. The as-prepared N-doped carbon quantum dot(N-CQDs) showed both photoluminescence exposed to UV radiation and a good potentiality in fluorescent sensor for detecting of ClO−. Significantly, the detection limit of ClO− was 1.47 μM and its linear range was 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9922). In addition, the structures and properties of the N-CQDs were further investigated. As indicated in the results, the N-CQDs exhibited a spheroidal morphology with an average diameter of 4.21 nm. Its quantum yield was 25.8%. Moreover, the N content of this N-CQDs increased to 9.89%, consisting of multiple functional groups such as N, CN and NH.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitin
Detection of ClO−
Fluorescent sensor
N-doped carbon quantum dots(N-CQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of chitin and N-CQDs. (B) XRD diffractogram of chitin and N-CQDs.
(A) XPS spectra of chitin and N-CQDs. (B) C1s high-resolution spectra of chitin and N-CQDs. (C) N1s high-resolution spectra of chitin and N-CQDs. (D) O1s high-resolution spectra of chitin and N-CQDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra and excitation, emission fluorescent spectra of NCQDs. (inset figure: the photographs of N-CQDs exposed to day light and a 365 nm UV lamp).
(A) Fluorescent emission spectra of N-CQDs at different range of excitation wavelengths. (B) Fluorescent emission spectra of N-CQDs at different pH values.

(A) The relative fluorescent intensities (F/F0) of N-CQDs added 300 μM various metal ions. (B) The relative fluorescent intensities (F/F0) of N-CQDs added 300 μM various anions. (C) Fluorescent emission spectra of N-CQDs added a series of concentrations of ClO−. (inset figure: inset figure: the photographs of N-CQDs absence and presence of 300 μM ClO− exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp) (D) The relative fluorescent intensities (F0/F) of N-CQDs added different concentration of ClO−. (inset figure: the linear relationship between F0/F and ClO− concentration (0–50 μM)).
(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of ClO−. (B) Fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of ClO−.

head.ce:title: Potentiality of carbon quantum dots derived from chitin as a fluorescent sensor for detection of ClO−

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chitin, a functional carbohydrate derived from waste products of seafood industry, contains abundant nitrogen, which makes it become a very suitable carbon precursor for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Demonstrated in this work was synthesizing CQDs using this N-enriched primary feed-stock. The as-prepared N-doped carbon quantum dot(N-CQDs) showed both photoluminescence exposed to UV radiation and a good potentiality in fluorescent sensor for detecting of ClO−. Significantly, the detection limit of ClO− was 1.47 μM and its linear range was 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9922). In addition, the structures and properties of the N-CQDs were further investigated. As indicated in the results, the N-CQDs exhibited a spheroidal morphology with an average diameter of 4.21 nm. Its quantum yield was 25.8%. Moreover, the N content of this N-CQDs increased to 9.89%, consisting of multiple functional groups such as N, CN and NH.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are a part of nanostructured carbon materials, were first discovered in the separation and purification of carbon nanotubes by Xu et al. [1]. Since then, this appealing carbon-based fluorescence nanomaterial has received considerable attention, thanks to their unique merits, such as superior optical properties, high dispersion stability, high chemical stability, and excellent photostability [2,3]. CQDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with typical cores from amorphous to nanocrystalline. Its diameter depends on the synthesis method, generally no more than 10 nm [4,5]. Up now, myriad methods to synthesize CQDs could be roughly classified into “top-down”, “bottom-up” and surface passivation and functionalization [5]. Among them, functionalization and surface passivation of CQDs, introduced various functional groups such as amines, carboxyl and sulfonic acid, played a vital role in improving their properties. Recently, many researchers have found that doping nitrogen in the surface of CQDs is helpful to improve the graphene structure and bandwidth-energy gap within them, which can not only increase fluorescence quantum yield but also improve optical properties, reactivity and catalytic activity [6]. Moreover, the obtained CQDs by sulfuric acid as the carbonization agent and dopant, exhibited extremely acidophilic high luminescence. It found that desirable functional groups formed through dehydration, carbonization and oxidation [7]. At present, heteroatom doped CQDs have been widely used for biological labeling, optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis and fluorescent sensor [8–10].

The carbon precursor for CQDs could be either artificial goods or natural products [11–13]. During the last decade, sparked tremendous attention has been paid to biomass carbon source, because it is renewable, abundance, low cost and eco-friendly. Carbohydrates like cellulose, potato, hemicellulose, corncob and rice husks have been shown to be precursors for the synthesis of CQDs, with fabulous optical properties [14–16]. Nevertheless, the CQDs with carbohydrates as precursors have low quantum yield (QY), less than 10%. Doping in N element could increase the QY of CQDs. Thereby, chitin containing abundant heteroatoms(N) seems to be a suitable raw material for the preparation of nitrogen doped quantum dots (N-CQDs). Chitin, the second abundant renewable carbohydrate polymers after cellulose, is a renewable carbohydrate widely present in seafood waste.[17–19]. Its production is approximately up to 1010–1011 tons per year. It is found that linear chitin chains are biosynthesized by linking N-acetyl-D-glucosamine units through 1–4 glycosidic bonds [20]. Thus, during the hydrothermal carbonation of chitin, nitrogen atom would be homogeneously incorporated into the carbocyclic rings, leading to obtaining high-quality N-CQDs. Transforming low-value biomass waste into valuable and useful materials could not only benefit environment but also create high added-values of chitin.[21–25].

There were few reports about how to use chitin as a precursor for preparation of CQDs. In these reports, chitin usually need some pretreatments for the removal of acetyl group or formation of chitin nanofibers [26,27]. Mechanical treatments have been mainly employed for chitin nanofibers preparation using high pressure homogenizers or ultrasonic disintegrators [25], which needs a lot of energy. Obviously, this pretreatment is uneconomic. Meanwhile, the deacetylation process needs under strong aqueous alkali and at a high temperature (100 °C). Usually, the con-centration of sodium or potassium hydroxides is used to be 30–50% w/v [28]. As is known to all, this process costs a lot and requires high alkali resistance of equipment. Besides, after this pretreatment the precursor for preparation of CQDs was actual a mixture of chitin and chitosan, but not chitin. In order to reduce costs, this work focused on regarding chitin serving as a remarkable precursor without any pretreatment for the synthesis of N-CQDs with ammonia as passivator. Ammonia could not only accelerate the degradation and carbonization of chitin during the hydrothermal treatment but also be employed as an extra nitrogen source. The as prepared N-CQDs showed both photoluminescence and a good potentiality in fluorescent sensor for detecting of ClO−. Thereby, it significantly provided a new way for improving the high value utilization of chitin.

Chitin (Acetyl content ≥ 90%) was purchased from Jiangsu Baiyao Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). Quinine sulfate dehydrates (99% purity) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ammonia solution (28%), sodium hypochlorite (available chlorine 5.5%) were obtained from Sinopharm Reagent Chemicals Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals and reagents were of analytical-reagent grades and used without further purification. Chitin was dried in a vacuum dryer at 40 °C for 24 h before used.

In short, 0.8 g Chitin was dispersed in of deionized water. Then, a certain amount of ammonia (mass ratio of chitin to ammonia = 1:10) was slowly added into the suspension. After blended evenly, the mixtures were transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave to undergo a hydrothermal treatment at 240 °C for 10 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the dark brown solution, indicating the formation of N-CQDs was further sonicated for 30 min, then centrifugated at 1000 rpm for 10 min to remove unresponsive products. After that, the larger particles were removed through 0.22 μm filter. The resultant supernatant dialyzed against deionized water (DW:300) for 48 h, in order to remove some small molecules formed in hydrothermal process.

The FT-IR spectra was recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 using a Perkin Elmer FTIR spectrometer. XRD spectra was carried out by using an Ultima IV diffractometer from 10° to 35° at a rate of 5°/min with a Ni-filtered Cu Kα1radiation (λ = 1.542 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was performed on an AXIS UltraDLD with Al X-rays at 150 W. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was collected by a UV1006M031 UV–vis spectrophotometer at a speed of 0.5°/min from 200 nm to 600 nm. The Fluorescence was measured by a LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The slit widths of excitation and emission are 10 nm and 2.5 nm, respectively. The morphology of products was characterized by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope.

The QY of N-CQDs was carried out with a method reported previously [29], using Quinine sulfate (QY is 54% in 0.10 mol/L H2SO4) as a reference. The calculation formula was as follows.
QY=Q
Y R
I S
I R
A R
A S
n S
n R
2
where I was on behalf of the integrated area under the luminescence, A was the absorbance at 360 nm and n stood for the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts R and S represented reference standard and N-CQDs products, respectively.

The selectivity of N-CQDs was an essential parameter for evaluating the performance of this fluorescent sensor. Thus metal ions, anions, including (Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+,Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Na+, Zn2+, Al3+, I−, SO3 2−, Cl−, NO2 −, NO3 −, ClO2 −, OH−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO3 2−, HSO3 −, SO4 2−) were examined. The specific method was as follows. 200 μL of different ions (300 μM) were added to 2 mL N-CQDs (0.02 mg·mL−1) adjusted pH to 3, respectively. After the mixture was slightly shaken for 1 min, the fluorescent intensity was measured using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.
Sodium hypochlorite solution was diluted into a series of concentrations (0–400 μM), then fluorescent intensity was measured as the method mentioned above.

Synthesis of the N-CQDs was based on degradation and carbonization of chitin during a hydrothermal treatment at 240 °C for 10 h with ammonia as a passivator. Ammonia, an alkaline solution, played an important role in accelerating the degradation and carbonization of chitin during the hydrothermal treatment. On top of this, during the hydrothermal treatment acetamido groups in chitin frameworks could decompose, which leading to a loss of N element. Thus, ammonia was also employed as an extra nitrogen source. As was shown at Table S1 thanks to the addition of ammonia, the yield and QY of the as-prepared N-CQDs increased to 17.6% and 25.8%.

Firstly, the FT-IR spectra was employed to analyze the chemical structures of the N-CQDs. As is showed in Fig. 1 A, the CH(2930 cm−1) bending vibrations evidently decreased and the adsorption of OH and NH stretching vibration shifted blue from 3440 cm−1 in chitin to 3293 cm−1 in the N-CQDs. This phenomenon could result from decomposition of the chitin chain and pyranose ring during the hydrothermal treatment [16]. Besides, it was worth noting that, in the infrared spectrum of the N-CQDs, an absorption peak at 1588 cm−1 presented. It indicated the formation of unsaturated CC bonds within carbon cores, which were consistent with other reports [14,30]. In addition, a typical stretching of CN appeared at 1395 cm−1. The presence of polar groups like amino group and hydroxyl were beneficial to improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the N-CQDs in aqueous systems.

Furthermore, the aggregation morphology of the N-CQDs were determined by XRD, which was showed in Fig. 1B. The XRD diffractogram of chitin exhibited an α chitin crystalline structure, emerging two characteristic peaks at 2θ = 12.6° and 21.6°, which coincided with the reflection of (0 2 1) and (1 1 0) planes, respectively [31]. But, there was only one broad diffraction peak at 23.0° in the as-prepared N-CQDs. This peak was similar to the (0 0 2) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with plentiful sp3 disorder.[32], It revealed that the N-CQDs were amorphous carbon phase structures with partial graphitization. These results implied during the hydrothermal treatment, carbon core structures that were comparable to that of graphite were gradually formed with the transformation of chitin.

Demonstrated in Fig. 2 was the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrums which was employed to characterize the chemical state and surface compositions of chitin and the as prepared N-CQDs. It indicated that, as for the N-CQDs, three peaks at 284.8, 399.6 and 531.8 eV correspond to C1s, N1s and O1s electrons, respectively, while three peaks linked with that in chitin occurred at 286.3 eV, 399.7 eV and 533.6 eV, respectively. Due to the participation of ammonia, the specific N elemental composition of the N-CQDs increased 3.49% compared with that of chitin (6.40%). In addition, the C/O ratio of the N-CQDs (3.27) was a little higher than that of chitin (1.52), which resulted from the degradation, and carbonization of chitin during the hydrothermal treatment [33]. There were four fitted peaks exhibited at 288.2 eV, 286.3 eV, 285.1 eV and 284.5 eV in the C1s high-resolution spectra of the N-CQDs, assigned to CO, CO, CN and CC/CC bonds [34], respectively. Meanwhile, the four peaks demonstrated in the C1s high-resolution spectra of chitin related to CO, CO, CN and CC bonds were at 288.1 eV, 286.5 eV, 285.5 eV and 284.7 eV, respectively. Then, appeared in N1s high-resolution spectra of the N-CQDs were three peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.6 eV and 400.5 eV, which could belong to the N, CN and NH bonds [35], respectively. While, that of chitin were deconvoluted into two peaks at 400.6 eV and 399.7 eV, which were relevant to CN and NH bonds, respectively. The other three peaks at 532.8/533.0 eV, 532.0/532.2 eV, 531.0/531.0 eV attributed to CO, COC and COH groups [36,37], respectively, emerged in the O1s high-resolution spectra of the N-CQDs/chitin. These analysis results of the C1s, N1s and O1s spectra were consistent with the FT-IR analysis.

The morphology of the N-CQDs was characterized by TEM, which was illustrated in Fig. 3 . During the hydrothermal process, chitin gradually degraded and carbonized to granule. As was indicated, the as-prepared N-CQDs presented a spheroid. Its diameter was about 4.21 nm, and ranged from 1 nm to 7 nm. The particle size estimation of TEM consistent with DLS data was illustrated in Fig. S2. From HRTEM, it was considerably apparent that the lattice spacing was approximately 0.21 nm, close to the (0 0 2) lattice spacing of graphite carbon [38], which was consistent with the XRD analysis.

Characterized by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were the optical properties of the as-prepared N-CQDs. As is shown in Fig. 4 , two obvious absorption peaks appeared at 213 nm and 272 nm in UV–Vis absorption spectrum, as a result of the n–π* transition of the CO band and π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band [39]. Moreover, it was obvious that the N-CQDs possessed excitation and emission peaks at 360 nm and 433 nm, respectively. The photographs inset in Fig. 4 exhibited the N-CQDs emerged light yellow under daylight and appeared brilliant blue fluorescence when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp.

The fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 310 nm to 410 nm was also illustrated in Fig. 5 A. The peaks of emission peak shifted to higher wavelengths with the increase of excitation wavelengths from 360 nm to 410 nm because of the excitation-dependent emission of N-CQDs. This important characteristic was mainly attributed to different surface groups on the N-CQDs or optical selection of nanoparticles with different sizes which was called quantum effect [40]. The fluorescence response of this N-CQDs in solution with different pH was also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the resultant N-CQDs surprisingly performed quite lower FL intensity in alkaline solution than that in acidic solution and the maximum intensity of was approached at pH3. Especially, when pH increased to 11, the fluorescence quenching of as-prepared N-CQDs became much more obvious, compared with which, that in pH1 was negligible. High pH values are likely to increase the negative charge density on the surface of N-CQDs, influencing the n-π* electronic transitions, thus resulting in PL quenching [38]. It indicated that this as-prepared N-CQDs have potential applications in an acid environment. On top of this, the effects of ionic strength on the N-CQDs was also studied, the results were shown in Fig. S1.

Hypochlorite has been widely used as a disinfectant in water treatments, such as drinking water, industrial water and wastewater treatment. In these treatments, the concentration of hypochlorite must be strictly controlled. Because the concentration is too low to purify and disinfect water completely, but too high not to do harm to water quality vis forming undesirable byproducts reacted with organic materials [41]. In this work, we succeeded in synthesizing N-CQDs with chitin as precursor, which showed a good potentiality in fluorescent sensor for detection of ClO−. Firstly, the selectivity of N-CQDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 A, B, among various metal ions and anions, it was obvious that only ClO− quenched the PL intensity of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, others showed lower or negligible effect. It indicated that the as-prepared N-CQDs showed a good selectivity to ClO− from other ions. The photographs (inset in Fig. 6C) showed that the N-CQDs turned from bright blue fluorescence to dark with 200 μL ClO− added (300 μM), under illumination with a luminescent lamp (365 nm). On top of this, the titration of ClO− by fluorescent spectroscopic experiments was also studied. The results were shown in Fig. 6C. A continuous decline in fluorescence intensity was found with the addition of different concentrations of ClO− (0–400 μM). The relationship between F0/F and the concentration of ClO− was shown in Fig. 6D. A nice linear correlation (R2 = 0.9922) was acquired, equation of which was F/F0 = 0.0032C + 1.0021 (C: the concentration of ClO−) with linear range of 0–50 μM. According to the equations LOD = 3σ/K (K: the slope of linear regression equation, σ: standard deviation), the LOD was calculated to be 1.47 μM (0.075 mg L−1), which was much lower than the maximum concentration of ClO− (4 mg L−1) at the distal end of water distribution system regulated by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) [42]. In conclusion, the as-prepared N-CQDs could be a specific fluorescent sensor for detection of ClO−. The comparison of this fluorescent method with previous works was illustrated in Supplementary data.

Generally, the fluorescence quenching mechanism could be classified into dynamic and static quenching. Dynamic quenching refers to the result of the collision between the excited state of the fluorescence group and the quenching agent. However static quenching was related to the combination of fluorescent substance and the quenching agent [33,43,44]. In order to better understand the quenching mechanism of ClO−, the analyses of fluorescence lifetime and UV–Vis absorption spectrum without and with the addition of ClO−, which were shown in Fig. 7 . The UV–Vis absorption peak at 213 nm decreased significantly, and that at 272 nm almost disappeared with the addition of ClO−. It could be linked with the interaction between the surface functional groups of the N-CQDs and ClO−. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was about 12.62 ns, shifting to 11.83 ns after adding ClO−. These results indicated that the quenching mechanism of ClO− belonged to a dynamic process [45].

In summary, N-CQDs were synthesized successfully by using chitin as a primary feed-stock and ammonia as passivator. It showed photoluminescence, appearing brilliant blue fluorescence when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp. Furthermore, this N-CQDs exhibited marvelous selectivity to ClO−, with a detection limit of 1.47 μM and a linear range was 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9922), which suggest carbon quantum dots derived from chitin possessed a superb potentiality in fluorescent sensor for detection.

Qiwen Jiang: Conceptualization Writing - original draft Formal analysis. Yi jing: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yeyan Ni: Methodology. Ruoshi Gao: Software. Pingyan Zhou: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31370583).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105111.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
